sentences.text: The thing that struck me was with this guide who was-- he had been there since he was a Signal Corps photographer-- and he had been all over the camp so he knew where things were.
One of the feelings that I had was that it was like a riot to me.
There was-- where-- see, we had not got into the main part of the camp yet.
We had been on the periphery.
When we got inside the camp on the other side of the moat, where I said there were some SS guys laying on the moat that evidently had been killed by the inmates-- they just were laying there dead.
And then there was the fence, barbed-wire fence.
And these inmates were-- they weren't fighting.
They were so happy.
And-- and-- and the Americans were being kissed and hugged and thrown up in the air and all this kind of stuff.
But the noise was-- was scary, like a football game, a roar, a roar, you know?
And I felt-- I only had a pistol.
That's all I was armed with, was a 45.
And I felt like I needed more firepower.
I ju-- for some reason this scared me.
It just-- it just scared me.
There were, you know, a couple of thousand people.
And you could see- I could see helmeted Americans in there.
And I knew they weren't in harm's way.
But they all had rifles with bayonets.
And some of them had machine guns.
So--
The Americans.
And they were-- they were in-- these were the liberators inside with the prisoners.
And what they were trying to do was calm everybody down.
You know, there were officers inside and-- and probably 150 American soldiers.
And they were trying to get the prisoners to calm down.
And they-- and I didn't know how they-- till later, how they did this.
They got the leadership of-- and there was-- there was an inter-- a structure just like there was in every camp-- to take over.
And-- and these were usually Jewish rabbis or people like that, and calm, calm down and get an order.
And-- and go back to your barracks.
And we'll sort-- we're going to bring food, and, you know, that kind of stuff.

So this is what the inmates were finally-- what finally happened was that the-- the leadership of the camp, which were chosen by the inmates-- and there some-- there were some priests, a few priests, quite a few rabbis in Dachau.
And kill-- they'd killed a couple hundred priests already from Poland, polish priests.
OK, I remember things subsiding.
And he said-- the driver.
I wish I could think of his name now.
He's dead.
And I'm in touch with his wife.
But anyhow he said, you've got to see the gas chamber.
And I said, the gas chamber?
They got-- and I hadn't even heard about the gas chambers yet.
He said, yeah and there's a crematorium part of the gas chamber.
And he said, wait, we'll be there.
So he drives his jeep and we drive over to this-- to this buil-- set of buildings.
There was about three buildings.
And-- and one of them has a sign in German that says-- and there's a picture of it in there-- that says Bad, which is bath I guess.
And there's also a warning sign, the international sign for death.
And he said, this is the gas chamber.
And OK, so we went in there.
And-- and it was just a room about like this, maybe a little bit bigger.
Concrete walls, concrete floors, concrete ceilings, shower heads.
It said [GERMAN] on the shower heads.
And then we left there and went to the next room, which was the crematorium, which still had the corpses in the partially decom-- cremated in the crematorium.
And in this-- in this crematorium, there was kind of like a sub floor.
And it was full of bits of cremated bodies, parts like little bone chips and stuff like that.

So evidently what they did when they were finished was they would just kind of shove all the ashes out one end and it would go through this grating in the sub floor, and then guys on the outside would shovel it into a cart or something and take it away.
I don't know.
They used it for manure, for-- for fertilizer, for slippery places.
It was-- you know, these were bodies.
These were people.
And they used it as kind of like gravel.
That's-- that's, you know, awesome to think about that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But later, by now it's getting late in the afternoon, maybe 3:30, 4 o'clock.
And things have quieted down considerable.
We had been to the train.
We'd been to the gas chamber.
He took me to-- OK.
This was the most-- the hardest thing for me.
And this is where I just-- I cracked up.
But we went into a subcamp of Dachau which was separated by gates and fences.
You couldn't get in, had double fences and, you know, the kind with a hook on them like this, which meant they were barbed-wire fence-- I mean electrified fences.
And-- and a double set of gates.
And we went in there.
And he had already been in there.
I  beg your pardon?
I haven't a clue what it was.
There were 40-some subcamps at Dachau.
And whether they all had names or not, I don't know.
But the only thing that separated this from the rest of the camp was the wire.
Otherwise you would have been in the same perimeters, because it was a camp within a camp basically.
So we go in there.
And there's about 40 barracks, buildings, barracks.
Typical brown barracks with roofs and that.
And they-- they-- they had doors.
The doors didn't close.
The windows were way up high.
And there was a space between the ceiling of the roof rather and the wall, like about like this.
So the wall came up here and the ceiling-- the roof came here.
And there was a space.
And I thought that's gl-- glass it's got.
And it wasn't.
But it wasn't glass.
It was just space.
These things weren't even air proof, wat-- or waterproof.
And this is the Bavaria.
God, they must have been miserable.
In this section of the camp, this guy said, now, you know, this is going to make you sick.
These guys are all dying.
The same thing as-- as in Buchenwald, only it was bigger.
So we go in there and the smell, the smell in here was much worse.
And there was-- there was moaning.
There were-- in the other part, in Buchenwald, I didn't hear anything.
This camp you could hear, in this part, you could hear murmurs.
You could hear feeble cries.
You could hear people trying to-- as we walked by you could hear people trying to get your attention.
And I said, let's go in a couple of these.
He said, they're all-- they're all quarantined.
I said, well, we're going to get sprayed anyway.
What the hell difference does it make, with the DDT.
And so we went in one.
And honest to God, I couldn't go any further than from here to you.
I had to get out of there, because it was much-- it was much worse than the one in Buchenwald, in the same sense that these people were dying or dead and nobody was paying any attention to them.
I mean, there-- there was no medics around.
And they had already evidently been in there and said, just forget this place.
We've got to help these other guys.
These guys are goners.
These guys maybe have a chance, so they just forgot about them.
So we got-- we walked back toward the gate.
And I see this little old guy sitting up against a barracks in the sun.
And this is April now, don't forget.
It had snowed the day before.
The night before.
And one of the things that was so weird about the train and the corpses in the train that was on-- in the open, was they were dusted with snow.
The ground had-- it wasn't thick.
It was just kind of a dusting, but it gave it the damnedest appearance.
Kind of like Dickens.
It reminded me of a Dickens Christmas tale, of the ghosts from the past or something like that.
It was just, just weird.
I'm going back to the train.
I shouldn't have done-- OK, so this little guy is sitting there, and he has on a little hat, a little cap with no-- no bill, but it's a striped cap.
And it's just a little square like, like a little square cap.
And the-- the uniform, the striped pants and shirt.
And he has some kind of a jacket on of some kind.
It was a raggedy torn, beat up thing.
And he's sitting there and is kind of going like this.
And I took out a Lucky and lit it and went over to him.
Just this was an impulse.
And I took a drag, and I-- and I-- he-- when I walked over, he saw my boots and he looked up.
And his eyes were all crusted, you know, from like a sick person's.
He had been drooling.
He smelled to high heaven.
But I took a drag and I tried to say, do-- how about here.
Here.
[INAUDIBLE].
I could speak Spanish, [SPEAKING SPANISH].
And he didn't say anything.
He took the cigarette, though.
And he looked at it.
And he-- he held it like this.
And he looked at it.
And he put it in his lips.
And he-- and nothing happened.
I could see his cheeks going.
But he wasn't able to get any smoke.

And-- and, you know, I mean he's And I'm thinking , Jesus, he's-- he's got that cigarette in his mouth and he can't puff on it, and I could see the smoke coming out the-- you know, out of the cigarette.
So then he looks up at me.
And tears rolled down his face.
And I just started to cry then.
And-- and-- excu-- I'll be all right.
I won't do it.
[SIGH].
But I to-- I had to get away.
I just-- I just had to get away from him.
I couldn't handle that.
I just couldn't handle that, that little old guy, couldn't even take a puff off of a cigarette.
And God it just gives me cold chills to think about that, that-- that-- The feeling was, you know, of-- of a mixture of rage and-- and frustration and why can't I-- why can't we do something?
You know, there's-- you-- you want to do something physical, because that's what we're used to doing.
Phys-- guys like him and I, you're used to doing physical stuff.
So you want to do something physical.
And what the heck.
Go do-- go do something physical.
So we-- we got out of there.
And I couldn't talk.
I told his guy, I said, I, you know, this is getting to be heavy duty for me.
And he said, well, let's go back to the main part of the camp then.
Well, then things have quieted down.
So we're coming down this street.
And there's a wall about this high.
And it goes clear around.
And this turned out to be the hospital, which I didn't know at the time.
And it goes-- we're here.
And the wall is here.
And there's an intersection, and then the wall continues like this.
So we're about 40 feet or so from the corner when we hear this machine gun fire.
And it was an American 30 caliber automatic.
I could identify it.
And it sounded like two of them.
So I said jeez, stop this jeep.
There's-- they're having a shoot out someplace.
So I got out of the jeep.
And I crawled on the ground to the corner.
And I peeked around the corner, because that's where the sound was coming from.
And here's a machine gun in the middle of the street, but just-- just, you know, not enough for two cars.
And another one up on the roof of a-- of a building across the street.
And in-- inside of this wall are these SS guys, 30, 40 of them.
And these two squads are just shooting them down, mowing them down.
And there's pictures in there of that.
So I thought, Jesus, you know, I could stop this.
You know?
I was a first lieutenant.
These-- and I couldn't tell who the other, the rank of these.
I could see one guy with sergeant stripes on.
There was a crew of four or five around each gun.
Ammunition, you know, and these are those machine guns that you feed with a belt, 30 caliber.
And so they-- then I saw an inmate jump over the wall with a 45.
And he goes over, and anybody that's still alive, he shoots them in the head.
Three or four-- three or four guys were still alive, I guess.
And he just pops them in the head.
And there's pictures of that in there too.
I didn't know what the hell to do.
I thought, you know, this is a war crime.
And these thoughts went through my mind.
But I didn't-- I wasn't saying we can't do this.
I didn't feel-- I didn't feel-- and I've thought about this a lot-- I didn't feel like I should stop it.
And I wasn't sure I could stop it.
I wasn't sure that if I did try that this guy wouldn't shoot me too, because they were nuts.
Then they brought out some more guys from the hospital, from what I found out later was the hospital, 30, 40.
And there's-- there's dead guy's laying there, and they lined them all up.
And these two squads shot-- finished them off.
And the inmates jumped over the wall again with 45s.
And anybody that was still alive they shot out there.
And there are pictures in there about this whole thing.
That really blew me away.
I mean, I did-- I was-- I was afraid, number one, for myself.
I-- I didn't feel any pity, per se.
I didn't think, those poor bastards.
You know?
They-- I knew-- I knew they were POWs, but I was so enraged that I did-- I-- I guess without giving it a lot of thought, I th-- I felt probably basically that those bastards deserved it.
And that's as far as it went, just on my part.
Can I interrupt for a moment?
Yes.
Americans.
Americans.
Two squads of Americans have mounted these 30-caliber machine guns, which is the smallest machine gun.
It's a weapon that has to be mounted on a tripod.
And there's one in the street, in this street.
You know, it's a street, but not a street like you'd think of as a street.
And there was a building across the street from-- from where the wall was.
And there's another gun on top of that, kind of like a mezzanine, with a crew shooting these German SS and [INAUDIBLE] guards.
What they had done, they had surrendered, taken them into the hospital for safekeeping, and as a place where they knew they could control them.
And this had all happened after the fire fight, you know, where the infantry had been fired on and had to kill these SS guards from the towers.
And they killed them because they were fired on.
They didn't kill him them because-- you know, the firefight lasted maybe 45 seconds.
But when they surrendered, they went ahead and killed them anyway.
These guys came down the stairs and-- and they-- they shot them.
I don't  know 20, 15, 20.
I don't know.
I don't really know.
But I didn't even think anything about that because I thought they had been in a firefight.
But this was a whole different situation here.
This was where they were shooting down unarmed men.
And like I say, in retrospect, I probably would have participated.
I don't think I could do it.
Gut wise, I don't think I have the guts to do that.
I don't know.
But I was enraged to the point where I didn't feel bad.

And when you-- you know, the thing that about that whole incident that bothered me the most was not the fact that they-- that they-- and thinking in retrospect, even today, was the-- the one man going over with a 45 and blowing another guy's head off.
I mean, that you could see.
You know, you could see him pick out the guy, because he was still moving, and put the gun and pull the trigger.
And you could see the guy's head pop like a watermelon.
And that-- that got me worse than watching them shoot them down in bunches, you know.
And OK, so that-- that-- tht incident was-- was-- that was pretty bad.
Later, I think-- I think I left the camp before sundown.
And here again we had-- had taken over-- they had taken over some houses.
And the in-- the infantry division-- there were two infantry divisions involved-- continued on.
They left some guards behind, 400 or 500 guards.
And-- but they continued on with their troops or tank support and what, to take Munich.
So most of them were gone the next day, the ones that I had been around with.
This guy that I had been with, he was gone.
And so we went back into the camp again, because we had the [INAUDIBLE] guy with us.
And they were meeting.

They were going on the tours with their photographers, with their Signal Corps photographers, taking their pictures and talking to these other generals, medical officers, whatever, about what could be done.
And they had to report back to Eisenhower.
So the rest of the time, the day, day and a half, two days, I don't know.
The smell was, I mean I just hated to go back in there.
I had no reason to go back in there.
I could have sit in the airplane.
We get to-- but I was getting schizy.
You know what I mean?
I was-- I was-- I was getting schizy.
I was thinking and talking to some of the other guys, you know, Jesus, using really bad, you know, the f-word and stuff like that.
And-- and we all were pretty b-- and we just didn't want to-- we would almost force ourselves to go back and-- and-- we wanted to try to find somebody we could talk to.
And we did find some.
We found some people who could speak English.
And one of the things that one of the guys was wanted to find out how the hell that gas chamber worked.
We couldn't find any water pipes.
Somebody said, hey, there's no water pipes to that damn gas chamber.
You know, if-- if that's a shower there has to be a source of water to go in there, or what's the point of having a shower with no water pipes?
And there weren't.
It was just, these were false shower heads, what we figured out.
So we went and we found some English-speaking inmates.
Now, things by now are beginning to be organized.
And the military had enough sense to let the structure of the camp itself organize it, because they could never-- never could have done it.
There's no way, without killing a bunch of people, could you have got that under control.
You had to let the inmates, the power structure, settle things down and take charge.
And that's exactly what they did.
And you had a lot of muscle there, but you couldn't do it.
You couldn't use it.
So the inmates were basically running the show pretty much.
We get ready to leave.
I had-- in the house that I was living, boy, I had a real nice house with a balcony.
And, or my room was where I could go out there.
And-- and I had my own bathroom, you know.
And I had-- I had my kit, my shaving kit and all that stuff.
But I'll be damned if I could get rid of that-- and I had a change, a new un-- a clean uniform, my before bag.
I changed.
I polished my shoes, because the shoes were all crappy.
I changed into an Eisenhower jacket and left my flight jacket in there, because it stunk to beat hell.
And I had that smell.
And I couldn't-- shampooed my hair with that brown G.I.
Soap, you know, and everything.
And even tried to dissolve some in water so I could pour over me thinking, boy, this stuff is like lye, you know.
And I still-- I still smelled.
I had some Mennen's shave lotion that I-- that my wife had sent me or somebody had sent me.
And I can remember putting that damn Mennen shaving lotion, that green stuff, gargling with it and spitting it out.
And it tasted like hell.
But I was just putting that all over me, you know, my hair and all over my shirt, between my toes.
We had G.I. foot powder for not athlete's foot, for trench foot.
Then we always put that inside of our socks.
So I'm putting all that stuff in there.
And I-- I went back in the camp two or three more times because I just had to do it.
I mean, and we talked about the gas chamber.
I told you that.
The-- the-- there was an execution chamber, a wall where-- it had-- it had been a firing range.
And they had set it up as the execution wall.
And they would-- they would take thousands in a day.
They would execute 1,500, 2,000 men, Russians, Russian POWs, for example.
All the Russian POWs were killed in Dachau.
They didn't-- and then Himmler heard about this and said, now, wait a minute.
And I read this in a book too, something I just got yesterday, I think.
That he felt that if things were getting too hairy, they better stop this.
So then they started evac-- well, they had already started evacuating the camp.
They had evacuated Buchenwald.
They had tried to evacuate Buchenwald before we got there.
They had evacuated a lot of men from Dachau.
And most of them had been killed or died en route.
They didn't-- and like the train had been shuttled back and forth to different camps.
Nobody would take the train, accept it.
We don't want the damn train.
So it-- it started at Dachau, and it went to someplace.
And they-- they said we don't want it.
So they shipped that for two weeks.
That's why all these guys died, is because they had no water, no food, no nothing except this damn train.
And they wouldn't-- and if you tried to get off, they shoot you, you know.
So that's how they died.
Nobody would accept the train, so they were getting panicky.
And [SIGHS] not Goebbels, head of the SS.
What the hell was his name?
I can't-- I can't think of it right now.
But he's the one that got nervous and told them-- they had orders to kill all the inmates of all the camps now.
I mean, as the Russians approached, as the Americans approached, as the British approached, as some of the French and Belgium troops approached the camps, the Canadians, kill the inmates.
Himmler said, no, don't do that.
He countermanded somebody else's orders.
And they-- and thank-- and thank God they didn't, or they would have killed another million, easy.
Um, [SIGHS] the death wall, the gas chamber.

You know, there were-- I asked, not only myself, but this other fellow, who said there was no plumbing, said to-- to one of the men that spoke English, who had been in the camp for six or seven years, which was amazing.
That camp was-- was-- was built in the '30s, for basic-- for political prisoners, Dachau.
It was the first camp.
And about the gas chamber, how did it work?
And so this man explained to us, and-- and he spoke English.
And it was-- you know, it was the same as the ones at-- at Auschwitz.
They had-- we found Zyklon B, by the way, at Dachau.
We found food, warehouses of food, at Dachau.
We found captured American rations, battlefield rations, at Dachau in warehouses.
So there was no reason for these people to be not-- to be malnourished.
There was food all over the place.
